title: Wellington HE324 Crash Site X Memorial
location_name: Wellington HE324 Crash Site, IWM-Registered Bomber Crash Memorials (Peak/Pennines)
narrative: This is one of the earliest crashes in the area and involves Polish airmen — men who had escaped occupied Poland to continue fighting from Britain. 301 Squadron was a Polish bomber squadron operating Wellingtons from Swinderby/Winthorpe. Returning from a night bombing raid on Bremen, the Wellington came down in a barley field at Winkburn. The heroism of the Broadberry family, who risked their lives to pull crew from burning wreckage, adds a powerful civilian dimension to this story.
cause_of_loss: Not confirmed — crash-landed in barley field
operation_target: Returning from bombing raid on Bremen

external_narratives.summary: War Memorials Online entry for the Wellington X HE324 crash site memorial. The memorial is a vertical square tablet with RAF badge and black lettering, dedicated in September 2013 after research to identify and notify surviving relatives of the six crew members.
key_details: Vertical square tablet with RAF badge
Crash occurred in a field near Horwood, North Devon
Aircraft was on a training exercise from RAF Silverstone

external_narratives.summary: IWM War Memorials Register entry for the Wellington HE324 crash memorial. Located half a mile east of Horwood church in North Devon, the stone of remembrance commemorates six airmen who died when the Wellington crashed in flames on 18 September 1943.
key_details: Located near Horwood church, North Devon, EX39 4PE
Aircraft was seen on fire over Lovacott and East Barton before crashing

external_narratives.summary: Aviation Safety Network database entry for the Wellington HE324 accident on 18 September 1943. Records the aircraft type, date, and circumstances of the fatal training flight crash.
key_details: Six crew from multiple Commonwealth nations killed
Crew included RAAF, RCAF, and RAFVR personnel

external_narratives.summary: Peak District Air Accident Research catalogues military aircraft crash sites across the Pennines region. While HE324 is listed among their records, the actual crash location was in North Devon rather than the Peak District.
key_details: Cross-referenced in Pennines crash site database
